items.title: AMDA Emergency Relief #55: Crisis in Ukraine, 11 May 2026
items.description:
Country: Ukraine
Source: Association of Medical Doctors of Asia

Please refer to the attached file.

Activity Report: Dynasty Medical Center (Ukraine) - December 2025 - March 2026

Known for providing around-the-clock medical services, Dynasty Medical Center is AMDA’s partner organization based in the eastern Ukrainian city of Kharkiv.

For this past winter, which happened to be one of the coldest to date, the center also extended support to local citizens by offering a space to warm themselves up after the central heating became unavailable due to power outages. It also introduced people to apartments equipped with heating if their houses had been damaged by air raids.

For the medical side of its work, the center has seen an increase in the number of seasonal illnesses among children during the winter months, including respiratory inflammation, ear infections, and adenoid hypertrophy, with some cases requiring surgical procedures. These illnesses were observed in addition to conflict-related injuries such as ruptured eardrums and facial injuries caused by explosions and bombardments.

To help people in need, the center also sees about 40 to 50 patients per month for free, with around 10 of them receiving surgical operations also free of charge.

Although people’s daily lives have been far from peaceful, the extent of the conflict can be limited in Kharkiv. Price hikes have been significant but essential goods such as medicines, medical items, foodstuffs, and daily necessities are still available. The household energy bill has remained relatively unchanged, while the energy bill for businesses has become twofold since the start of 2026. Public transportation services have been available for free, and although the city must deal with frequent power outages, the authorities have been trying their utmost to restore them, helping the city maintain order.

When asked how people’s lives have been in Kharkiv, Dynasty’s director commented: “We are trying to spend each day as normally as possible. People here enjoy strolling around the decorated city during the Christmas season, seeing friends or going cross-country skiing in places that are landmine-free. Times are tough but we treasure each day and live it as if it were always the same.”

items.title: Qatar Charity Distributes New Relief Supplies to Displaced Families in Sudan [EN/AR]
items.description:
Country: Sudan
Source: Qatar Charity

Please refer to the attached files.

Doha –11 May 2026

Qatar Charity (QC) has implemented a new humanitarian intervention for internally displaced persons (IDPs) from El Fasher and Greater Darfur in Sudan’s Khartoum State. Supported by a Qatari donor, the organization’s field teams in Omdurman delivered food, shelter, and non-food items (NFIs) to hundreds of families.

Wada’a Al-Amin, Representative of the Khartoum State Commission for Voluntary and Humanitarian Work (CVHW), acknowledged Qatar Charity's significant support for those fleeing the conflict. He noted that the aid distribution targeted around 400 displaced families from El Fasher, under the supervision of CVHW to ensure that aid reached those most in need.

Displaced individuals from El Fasher praised Qatar Charity and the donors of Qatar for their ongoing efforts in easing the humanitarian crisis. Essential aid, including food, blankets, medicines, and hygiene kits, was distributed to IDPs fleeing to Khartoum State.

Al-Wafid Al-Sir, Representative of the Darfur IDP Committee in Khartoum State, expressed his gratitude to Qatar Charity for providing humanitarian assistance to families displaced from various Darfur states. He noted that over 22,000 displaced persons from Darfur have been registered in Khartoum, all of whom require further humanitarian aid to overcome this ordeal. He voiced his hope for the cessation of the causes of displacement and for the safe return of all IDPs to their original homes.

Tariq Mohieddin, Acting Country Director of Qatar Charity’s Sudan Office, revealed that the distribution included 470 food baskets containing essential supplies, 1,000 blankets, 500 personal hygiene kits, and significant quantities of essential medicines.

He further explained that during the current month, Qatar Charity has implemented a large-scale project featuring four humanitarian convoys for El Fasher’s displaced populations in Al-Dabbah. These convoys focused on providing essential food, livelihoods, healthcare, and dignified housing. He added that high-impact humanitarian projects will continue to be implemented across several states in the coming period.

** End

items.title: Indonesia: ASEAN Weekly Disaster Update Week 19 | 4 – 10 May 2026
items.description:
Countries: Indonesia, Cambodia, Malaysia, Philippines, Viet Nam
Source: ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

REGIONAL SUMMARY:

During the nineteenth week of 2026, a total of 45 disaster events were reported across the ASEAN region, including floods, landslides, storms, volcanic activity, and wind-related disasters in Cambodia, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Viet Nam. In Cambodia, National Committee for Disaster Management (NCDM) reported wind-related disasters in Oddar Meanchey. In Indonesia, Badan Nasional Penanggulangan Bencana (BNPB) reported disaster events across Aceh, Banten, Bengkulu, Jakarta, Jambi, West Java, West Kalimantan, North Maluku, South Sulawesi, Central Sulawesi, Southeast Sulawesi, West Sumatra, South Sumatra, and North Sumatra. In Malaysia, Agensi Pengurusan Bencana Negara (NADMA) reported floods in Johor, Kedah, and Melaka. As of this reporting period, flooding was still being reported in Perak (Larut, Matang, and Selama) and Kedah (Kulim). In the Philippines, National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (NDRRMC) reported an M6.0 earthquake in Eastern Samar. Meanwhile, in Thailand, Department of Disaster Prevention and Mitigation (DDPM) reported storms and wind-related disasters in Ang Thong, Bueng Kan, Chaiyaphum, Chanthaburi, Chiang Mai, Kamphaeng Phet, Lampang, Lamphun, Lopburi, Mae Hong Son, Nakhon Ratchasima, Nakhon Sawan, Nan, Pathum Thani, Phetchabun, Phichit, Phitsanulok, Phrae, Rayong, Saraburi, Sing Buri, Tak, Udon Thani, and Uttaradit. Lastly, in Viet Nam, Viet Nam Disaster and Dyke Management Authority (VDDMA) reported storms and wind-related disasters in Dong Thap, Lao Cai, Tuyen Quang, and Phu Tho.

HIGHLIGHT:

In Indonesia, flooding occurred in Musi Rawas Utara Regency (South Sumatra), following moderate to heavy rainfall on Thursday, 7 May 2026 at 0400H UTC+7, affecting 4 districts, namely Karang Jaya, Rupit, Karang Dapo, and Rawas Ilir districts. According to BNPB, as of 10 May 2026 at 2045H UTC+7, one fatality was reported, while approximately 14.2K households (54.3K people) were affected. Around 14.2K houses were affected, including approximately 35 houses heavily damaged, 11 moderately damaged, and 101 slightly damaged. Reported damages also included five bridges, 16 health facilities, 44 educational facilities, 35 places of worship, 11 inundated office buildings, 27 inundated fishponds, 30 hectares of rice fields, and 850 hectares of plantations. The Regent of Musi Rawas Utara declared an Emergency Response Status for Hydrometeorological Disaster for the period of 7–20 May 2026. Urgent needs include fiberglass boats, boat engines, folding beds, life jackets, blankets, ready-to-eat food, clothing, bridge repairs, and reconstruction of damaged houses. As of the latest update, floodwaters in Karang Jaya District had receded, water levels in Rupit District had started to decrease, floodwaters from Kerta Sari Village to Karang Dapo in Karang Dapo District had begun to recede although Biaro Village remained inundated, Rawas Ilir District was still flooded, and the Rupit–Rawas road access up to Mandi Village was passable. Relevant authorities are undertaking necessary actions to respond to the situation, support affected communities, and continue assessing and consolidating impact and damage data.

HYDRO-METEO-CLIMATOLOGICAL:

For the past week, data from the ASEAN Specialised Meteorological Centre (ASMC) indicated medium to high 7-day average rainfall across Brunei Darussalam, Cambodia, Lao PDR, Indonesia, Malaysia, Myanmar, the Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Viet Nam. As of this reporting period, Tropical Cyclone HAGUPIT was moving generally northwestward and was forecast to weaken into a remnant low by the evening of 11 May or early morning of 12 May 2026.

GEOPHYSICAL:

Four (4) significant earthquakes (M>5.0) were recorded by Indonesia’s Badan Meteorologi, Klimatologi, dan Geofisika (BMKG) and the Philippines Institute of Volcanology and Seismology (PHIVOLCS). Mount Marapi (alert level II), Semeru (alert level III), Dukono (alert level II), and Ibu (alert level II) in Indonesia, and Taal (alert level 1), Mayon (alert level 3), Bulusan (alert level 1), and Kanlaon (alert level 2) volcanoes in the Philippines reported recent volcanic activity according to Pusat Vulkanologi dan Mitigasi Bencana Geologi (PVMBG) and PHIVOLCS.

OUTLOOK:

According to the ASEAN Specialised Meteorological Centre (ASMC), for the coming week, wetter conditions are forecast over most of the equatorial region for the coming week, while drier-than-usual conditions are expected over parts of the eastern half of Mainland Southeast Asia. Warmer-than-usual temperatures are also predicted across most of southeastern Mainland Southeast Asia and the northeastern Maritime Continent. ENSO-neutral conditions are present, with most models predicting a transition to El Niño conditions during June–July 2026. However, there remains a possibility that ENSO-neutral conditions will continue. Meanwhile, the Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) remains neutral, with models predicting the neutral phase to persist until June 2026.

Sources:

ASEAN Disaster Monitoring & Response System (DMRS); ASEAN Disaster Information Network (ADINet); ASEAN Specialised Meteorological Centre (ASMC); ASEAN Earthquake Information Centre (AEIC); Joint Typhoon Warning Centre (JTWC);

Cambodia: NCDM;

Indonesia: BNPB, BMKG, PVMBG;

Malaysia: NADMA,

Philippines: NDRRMC, PAGASA, DSWD, PHIVOLCS;

Thailand: DDPM;

Viet Nam: VDDMA;

Various news agencies.

items.title: Diagnosing return — Health conditions and experiences of returnees in Syria [EN/AR]
items.description:
Country: Syrian Arab Republic
Source: Relief International

Please refer to the attached files.

Leaders must address Syria’s public health crisis warns Relief International report

International non-profit Relief International calls on officials convening in Brussels to chart a roadmap for recovery that centers Syrians’ health and wellbeing as millions return home — many living with untreated injuries, chronic disease, and trauma.

  • Relief International’s report draws on a needs assessment funded by the European Union (EU) involving 2,109 people and a separate survey of 705 returnees across Aleppo, Deir-ez-Zor, and Rural Damascus governorates - The report demonstrates that health needs are increasing with malnutrition prevalent across all regions, 86% of women reporting anxiety and psychological distress, and an estimated 28% of Syrians now living with some form of disability according to the UN, which is nearly double the global average - At the same time, Syria’s decimated health infrastructure is under mounting strain with 78% of returnees in rural Deir-ez-Zor reporting that healthcare is unavailable according to Relief International’s findings

Damascus, Syria — Relief International launched a report today calling for urgent, coordinated action to rebuild Syria’s health system. The international non-profit warns that amid the influx of families returning home, patients are unable to access the care they need. This call comes as top Syrian and EU officials convene in Brussels for their first political dialogue regarding the country’s socio-economic recovery.

Since December 2024, more than three and a half million Syrians have returned to their homes, both from neighboring countries and within Syria. Many are dealing with increased health needs caused by war and worsened by disrupted care.

According to Relief International’s report, 86% of women surveyed said they were experiencing anxiety and psychological distress, driven by exposure to conflict and prolonged displacement. Upon return, these issues are further exacerbated by the near absence of maternal and reproductive care, as well as a lack of services for survivors of gender-based violence and a nationwide gap in mental health support.

Upon returning to rural Damascus, a displaced woman named Sawsan said, “The moment I came back from Idlib, I cried. This is not the home we left behind. There are no proper health services. Even seeing a doctor costs more than what people can afford. We came back to rebuild our lives. Even if we start from zero, we will try again.”

As well as mental scars, 14 years of conflict have left communities with undetected illness, untreated disease, and acute malnutrition. In the town of Al-Tibni in Deir-ez-Zor, where Relief Internationaloperates a mobile clinic, 100% of child screened were diagnosed with moderate acute malnutrition.

Petros Passas, Country Director for Relief International in Syria, explained: “Worryingly, only 10% of children under five had been screened in the first place, pointing to a nutrition crisis in Al-Tibni that is far greater than current data reflects. This high prevalence of malnutrition is consistent with conditions across the region and if left unaddressed, children will suffer irreversible consequences.”

Additionally, an estimated 28% of Syrians now live with some form of disability according to UN data. This is nearly double the global average, with many disabilities caused by war. The figure continues to rise amid landmine and unexploded ordnance contamination, yet rehabilitation services are among the most severely under-resourced in the country.

Relief International, which has supported more than ten thousands of Syrian refugees with disabilities living in Türkiye, says that specialized care is critical for long-term recovery. To this end, the organization’s local partner National Syrian Project for Prosthetic Limbs (NSPPL) is linking refugee patients in Türkiye with service providers inside Syria to ensure that case management can be sustained upon return.

Mr Passas continued: “This cross-border model is the kind of continuity-of-care infrastructure that must be built at scale if return to Syria is to be safe, dignified, and durable for those most in need.”

Relief International is supporting over 50 health facilities to deliver integrated health, nutrition, and water services across Syria, including mobile clinics in underserved areas. While actors like Relief International are committed to strengthening Syria’s health system, the organization says a concerted, global effort is required to help the country stabilize and recover.

Addressing leaders meeting in Brussels today, Mr. Passas said: “People are living in and returning to areas where hospitals are destroyed, health workers have fled, medicines are unaffordable, and equipment is out-of-date. Without large-scale and sustained commitment from the international community, Syrians face a very real public health crisis.

“At this moment of hope and transition for Syria, the support of all governments, donors, and actors with power remains crucial. This first high-level political dialogue between Syrian and EU officials is an opportunity to reduce spiraling needs and restore access to essential health services for all Syrians.

“Our ask today is clear: seize this moment to chart an actionable roadmap for recovery that centers Syrians’ health and wellbeing. Sustained investment, an enabling political environment, and support for the Syrian Ministry of Health’s Strategic Plan are critical components to the way forward. Our collective approach must be community-led, inclusive, and based on the priorities of those most affected by the conflict — including women, young people, and displaced populations. Healing Syria begins with healthy Syrians.”

Ends

For more information, please contact frankie.harrington@ri.org / +44 (0)7801 797 808

About Relief International

Relief International is an international non-profit organization working in 14 countries across Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. We partner with communities impacted by conflict, climate change, and disaster to save lives, build greater resilience, and promote long-term health and wellbeing.

items.title: Burundi: Monthly update - DRC Situation: 1 - 28 February 2026
items.description:
Countries: Burundi, Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

Hignlights

• UNHCR commends the Government of Burundi for its leadership, steadfast engagement and solidarity following the emergency influx of over 101,000 people in December 2025, enabling protection, access to land, and the rapid delivery of life-saving assistance at the Busuma refugee site. UNHCR also recognizes the critical support of the UN Resident Coordinator in driving coordination, advocacy, and resource mobilization, alongside the commitment of UN agencies, and humanitarian partners, whose collective eorts have significantly strengthened and continue to enhance the response to newly arrived refugees.

• As of 28 February, 66,727 newly arrived refugees, representing 14,061 households, were hosted at the site in Ruyigi in Buhumuza Province. Children account for 59 per cent of the population, underscoring significant protection concerns and needs

• On 23 February, Burundi reopened the Gatumba–Kavimvira land border with the Democratic Republic of Congo. This development has raised expectations among some refugees regarding potential return. In collaboration with the Government of Burundi, UNHCR protection teams regularly undertake joint cross-border assessments to monitor the situation and keep refugees informed. A joint communiqué issued by the Government and UNHCR encourages refugees to wait, emphasizing that any return must remain voluntary, informed and based on free choice.

• 9,913 refugees remain in overcrowded transit centres, in Cishemere Transit Centre (8,132 people) and Makombe Transit Centre (1,781 people) Urgent support is needed to provide safer shelter, adequate food assistance, and essential services, particularly for the most vulnerable

items.title: UNHCR Romania Ukraine Refugee Situation Update - 11 May 2026
items.description:
Countries: Romania, Ukraine
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

Highlights

On 6 May, UNHCR participated in a round table entitled “The New Architectures of Digital Democracy”, organized by the Presidential Administration in the context of Europe Week.
The event brought together public institutions, civil society, academia and the technology sector to discuss the impact of artificial intelligence and digital transformation on democratic processes. During the discussions, UNHCR underscored the importance of ensuring that increasingly digital systems and public services remain accessible to vulnerable groups. It also highlighted that the more than 120 million forcibly displaced people worldwide represent one of the fastest-growing populations globally, while remaining among the least represented in democratic and digital spaces.

items.title: Drought and displacement push millions to the brink in Somalia and Ethiopia’s Somali Region
items.description:
Countries: Somalia, Ethiopia
Source: Médecins Sans Frontières

Four consecutive failed rainy seasons have driven a severe drought emergency across Somalia, pushing millions into acute food insecurity and forcing millions more from their homes. As humanitarian funding collapses, the last remaining lifelines are now at risk of being cut.

In November 2025, Somalia’s federal government declared a national drought emergency. More than 6.5 million people, approximately one in every four Somalis, now face high levels of acute food insecurity, according to the widely used integrated food security phase classification (IPC). More than two million people are in IPC Phase 4, indicating extreme food gaps and a high risk of malnutrition and death. Over 1.84 million children under five are expected to suffer acute malnutrition in 2026 in the country.

“We were displaced because of the drought,” says Regay Ali a displaced person who left her home in Weelbelil, around 160 kilometers from Baidoa southwest state, Somalia. She borrowed money from neighbours to reach a displacement camp in Baidoa town. “We do get water, but it’s not enough: two jerrycans – per day - for washing, bathing, cooking and drinking. Even five would not be enough. Hunger is weighing heavily on us. We were displaced because of hunger, and where we are now, we still don’t have enough.”

Approximately 3.3 million people like Regay are internally displaced across Somalia, driven by drought and conflict, and more than 50,000 have crossed into Ethiopia in search of water and assistance according to UNHCR. Displacement sites around Baidoa and Galkayo are filling rapidly; water prices have risen beyond what most families can afford. The risk of water-borne diseases is also rising due to high numbers of people using the same limited - and at times - unsafe water sources. According to UNOCHAi, in Puntland, where Galkayo is located, approximately 170 boreholes and shallow wells were non-functional as of December 2025, severely limiting access to safe water for communities already under extreme pressure.

At MSF’s facility in Baidoa, we are already recording an alarming number of severely malnourished children, with the hospital overwhelmed and treating patients beyond its capacity. This sharp rise at the very start of the lean season signals a worsening situation in the months ahead.

The consequences of failed rainfalls go beyond Somalia’s borders. According to Ethiopia’s Somali Region GU (Rainy Season in East Africa) seasonal need assessment report 2025, the region is experiencing the same pattern as similar rainfall deficits have severely affected pastoral and agropastoral communities. In the south of the Ethiopia’s Somali region, particularly in Afder Zone and Shebelle Zone, dry lowlands near the Somalia border, repeated insufficient rains have resulted in livestock losses, acute water shortages and rising food insecurity. Pastoralist communities have lost their livelihoods, and competition over scarce water resources is increasing.

“Most people in this community were rearing livestock: that was how we survived. When the rain stopped, we lost our livestock, and people fled to wherever they could find water to survive,” says Isaq Ibrahim Mohamed, a resident of Barey District in Afder Zone. “Our lives are so harsh, because there is nothing to depend on. People walk an hour or more just to fetch water from rivers, and we share it with the animals. We see diarrhoea and malnutrition.”

During an assessment led by the Ethiopia’s Somali Region Health Bureau in collaboration with MSF in both Afder and Shebelle zones in Ethiopia’s Somali region, our teams identified urgent gaps in water and nutrition services, as local health capacity is overstretched. This situation is aggravated as more medical and humanitarian organizations withdraw due to funding shortages, while rising fuel prices linked to the escalation of conflict in the Middle East and limited supply chain movement further constrain the response.

“From the areas we assessed with the regional health bureau, we saw a high number of malnutrition admissions in the existing facilities. What we are seeing on the ground is a reduction of the services that patients previously received, as partners scale back due to global funding cuts and shortages. This has created a heavy burden on the existing system. Water and sanitation programs are the ones most affected,” says Abdullahi Mohammad Abdi, deputy medical team leader in MSF Ethiopia. Here, MSF is collaborating with local health authorities on nutrition and wash activities in Barey district, Afder zone. MSF is also planning to expand the support to Shebelle zone.

In Somalia, MSF has been responding to the drought emergency since December 2025. In Baidoa, more than 30 million litres of safe water have been distributed to over 21,000 people across 17 displacement sites. In Mudug, MSF is providing three million litres of drinking water, sanitation and hygiene support to nearly 11,000 people near Galkayo, including borehole rehabilitation and the distribution of hygiene kits.

But just as the needs are increasing, global funding support has collapsed. Somalia’s Humanitarian Needs and Response Plan for 2026 is funded at only 10.9 per cent according to UNOCHA. World Food Program has cut emergency food assistance from over two million people to just over 600,000: only one in seven Somalis who needs food assistance is now receiving it. More than 300,000 people have lost access to safe water as a direct result of this underfunding, and over 70 health facilities in Puntland have closed.

“What we are witnessing across the displacement sites is a scale of need that exceeds what any single organization can address alone,” says Mohammed Omar, MSF head of program in Somalia. “People are arriving every day, and resources are not keeping pace. We call on the international community and governments to urgently step up their support now, with sustained and flexible funding, before more lives are lost to entirely preventable causes.”

MSF calls on donors to immediately restore and increase funding for the humanitarian response across Somalia and Ethiopia's Somali Region, where four consecutive failed rainy seasons have pushed millions to the edge of survival. The withdrawal of aid at this moment is not inevitable. It is a choice, and it is costing lives.

items.title: UNAIDS pays tribute to Botswana’s former President Festus Mogae, a champion, pioneer and leader in the response to HIV
items.description:
Country: Botswana
Source: UNAIDS

**GENEVA, 11 May 2026—**UNAIDS mourns the death of Botswana’s former president Festus Mogae. President Mogae led Botswana between 1998 and 2008, tackling Botswana’s HIV pandemic with resolve and dedication as the country faced one of highest HIV burdens in the world.

Under his leadership, Botswana became the first African country to launch a national, free and comprehensive HIV treatment programme, setting a precedent for the region and the world. During President Mogae’s tenure, AIDS-related deaths were reduced by 39% and new HIV infections among children were reduced by 73%, putting Botswana well on the path to ending AIDS. In 2021, Botswana became the first high burden country in the world to reach the Path to eliminating mother to child transmission of HIV.

He understood early that ending AIDS required more than medicine alone. He consistently called on leaders to confront stigma, discrimination and inequality, recognizing that protecting human rights was essential to protecting public health.

“I am deeply saddened by the passing of former President Festus Gontebanye Mogae,” said Winnie Byanyima, Executive Director of UNAIDS. “He was a courageous and visionary leader who confronted HIV with honesty, science and compassion when few dared to do so. At a time when many questioned whether African countries could deliver universal HIV treatment, President Mogae demonstrated that bold political leadership, national ownership and investment in people could change the course of an epidemic. His legacy lives on in the many lives saved and changed and in the global AIDS response he helped shape. May he rest in power and peace.”

President Mogae remained a strong advocate for the AIDS response after his time in office. He devoted his time and voice to urging governments to lead with courage, compassion and accountability. In 2008 he launched, and served as Chairman, of the Champions for an HIV-Free Generation, a distinguished group of former presidents and influential African leaders committed to achieving an AIDS-free generation in Africa. He was also a member of UNAIDS High-Level Commission on HIV Prevention and member of the UNAIDS-Lancet Commission on Defeating AIDS–Advancing Global Health.

In 2008, President Mogae was awarded the Mo Ibrahim Prize for Achievement in African Leadership in 2008, including for his outstanding leadership on HIV response in Botswana and the rest of the African continent.

During his presidency, President Mogae led and championed an inclusive national HIV response, including access to treatment, fighting stigma and ending discrimination. President Mogae leaves an undisputed HIV legacy, which carried through Botswana’s successive leaders in the fight against the epidemic. UNAIDS joins the Government of the Republic of Botswana, its people and his family and the entire world in paying tribute to president Mogae.

UNAIDS

The Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS) leads and inspires the world to achieve its shared vision of zero new HIV infections, zero discrimination and zero AIDS-related deaths. UNAIDS unites the efforts of 11 UN organizations—UNHCR, UNICEF, WFP, UNDP, UNFPA, UNODC, UN Women, ILO, UNESCO, WHO and the World Bank—and works closely with global and national partners towards ending the AIDS epidemic by 2030 as part of the Sustainable Development Goals. Learn more at unaids.org and connect with us on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram and YouTube.

items.title: Bangladesh: Families urged to vaccinate children as number of suspected measles deaths exceeds 400
items.description:
Countries: Bangladesh, Myanmar
Source: Save the Children

DHAKA, 11 May 2026 - More than 400 people in Bangladesh have died from confirmed or suspected measles in the past two months with government data estimating the number of cases at about 55,000 as Save the Children urges families to ensure children are part of an emergency vaccination campaign [1].

Nine out of 10 cases are in children between the ages of one and 14 years, according to the World Health Organization, with about three quarters of infections occurring in under-fives [2].

In Cox’s Bazar, home to about 1 million Rohingya refugees, there have been four suspected deaths and nearly 500 confirmed or suspected cases, according to the Health Cluster. Of the 60 laboratory-confirmed cases so far reported in the camps in 2026, three quarters were either unvaccinated or partially vaccinated [3].

In 2025, there were 125 cases of measles in total across Bangladesh, according to the BMJ.

The measles outbreak is affecting all eight divisions in Bangladesh, prompting an emergency vaccination campaign across the country. In the last month, nearly 18 million children have been reached with at least one dose of the vaccine, according to government data [1].

Measles is a highly contagious disease which affects individuals of all ages and is one of the leading causes of death among young children globally, especially for children who are malnourished or have weakened immune systems.

It is almost entirely preventable with two doses of a vaccine, administered from the age of nine months, but Bangladesh had a severe shortage of measles vaccines, with stock outs in 2024 and 2025 leading to gaps in immunisation.

Shumon Sengupta, Country Director, Save the Children Bangladesh said:

“Measles is almost entirely preventable and is it devastating that so many children have lost their lives because of a lack of vaccines.

“It is vital that gaps in supply and routine immunisations are addressed long term, not just for the duration of the nationwide measles-rubella vaccination campaign.

“Before this outbreak, Bangladesh had made significant and commendable progress towards eliminating measles. All children and their families must have uninterrupted access to life-saving vaccines.”

In April, the Government of Bangladesh, UNICEF, WHO, and Gavi launched an emergency Measles Rubella (MR) vaccination campaign in response to the spike in cases among children and infants.

This is the first national measles campaign in Bangladesh since 2020, due to disruptions caused by the global COVID-19 pandemic, followed by recent political instability in the country.

Save the Children has implemented extensive activities focusing on vaccination support, community awareness, and capacity building.

The humanitarian and child rights organisation is encouraging families to register for vaccines and trying to ensure that eligible children are immunised through awareness campaigns run by a network of field staff and hundreds of volunteers.

The campaign includes door-to-door visits and collaboration with community groups, including support groups for mothers, and schools where children are encouraged to raise awareness of measles in their communities.

Save the Children has been working in Bangladesh for 55 years. Together with government, civil society organizations and businesses, we respond to major emergencies, deliver development programmes and ensure that children’s voices are heard through our campaigning to build a better future.

References
[1] https://dghs.gov.bd/pages/press-releases Data as of 10 May 2026.

[2] https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news/item/2026-DON598

[3] Data from the Health Cluster in Cox’s Bazar, Bangladesh, 7 May 2026

items.title: World: IFRC 2025 International Bilateral Aid Report – TRC’s Global Ranking
items.description:
Country: World
Source: Turkish Red Crescent Society

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

According to the 2025 International Bilateral Assistance Report published by the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, the Turkish Red Crescent ranked:

? First globally in the number of countries reached with humanitarian assistance, and

? Second globally in the total volume of international bilateral humanitarian assistance delivered.

In 2025, the Turkish Red Crescent provided a total of 119 million CHF in international humanitarian assistance, including:

▪️ 79 million CHF for Syria

▪️ 26 million CHF for Palestine/Gaza

▪️ 14 million CHF for other countries

In addition:

? 74% of international assistance delivered to Syria by National Societies was provided by the Turkish Red Crescent.

? 48% of assistance delivered to Palestine was provided by the Turkish Red Crescent.

Furthermore, the Turkish Red Crescent contributed 26% of the total 451 million CHF in international assistance delivered globally by National Societies

items.title: World: Voice out loud #40: Fragility: The Cost of Inaction (December 2025)
items.description:
Country: World
Source: Voluntary Organisations in Cooperation in Emergencies

Please refer to the attached file.

The latest issue of the VOICE out loud magazine examines why sustained engagement in fragile contexts is essential, even amid political and financial pressures. Through concrete experiences from humanitarian and development actors, it highlights the human, economic and geopolitical costs of disengagement, and offers practical insights for operationalising the Humanitarian–Development–Peace Nexus in fragile and conflict-affected settings.

items.title: UNHCR Rwanda - Total Forcibly Displaced Population Figures - 30 April 2026
items.description:
Countries: Rwanda, Burundi, Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

items.title: Sudan: Soudan : face à l’ampleur de la crise humanitaire, Médecins du Monde ouvre un programme humanitaire dans l’ouest du pays
items.description:
Country: Sudan
Source: Médecins du Monde

Depuis 3 ans, le Soudan est déchiré par une guerre civile d'une ampleur et d'une violence inouïes. 13 millions de personnes ont été déplacées, contraintes de fuir les combats. Médecins du Monde alerte sur l’urgence de la situation et lance des activités au Darfour, à l’ouest du pays.

Le Darfour figure parmi les zones les plus violemment impactées par les conséquences du conflit. Des millions de personnes déplacées dans des zones arides, difficiles d’accès, survivent dans des conditions catastrophiques. La population doit faire face à la famine – avec près de 5 millions d’enfants et de femmes enceintes ou allaitantes qui souffrent de malnutrition aiguë – et aux épidémies alors que 80 % des services de santé primaires ne fonctionnent plus.

Pour faire face à cette situation dramatique, Médecins du Monde a repris la gestion complète d’une clinique dans le camp de déplacés de Dar Omo, abritant 45 000 personnes. Dans cette zone géographiquement isolée, marquée par des contraintes logistiques majeures, Médecins du Monde est l’un des rares opérateurs à maintenir une présence active.

« Depuis l’ouverture de la clinique, Médecins du Monde réalise près de 200 consultations par jour, principalement pour des soins de santé primaires : nutrition, vaccination. Actuellement, 50 % des consultations pédiatriques se font pour des affections évitables telles que la pneumonie ou les diarrhées.

Nous nous préparons également à la gestion d’épidémies comme la rougeole ou le choléra, endémique dans cette région pendant la saison des pluies de juin à septembre, » explique Marie Laure HERDHUIN, responsable des Opérations d’Urgences pour Médecins du Monde.

Avec plus de 80 salariés nationaux, la clinique permet ainsi un accès aux soins de santé primaires, l’accès aux soins de santé sexuelle et reproductive, la prévention et la réponse aux épidémies et la prise en charge de la santé mentale.

items.title: World: Attacks on Health Care Bi-Monthly News Brief, 15 - 28 April 2026
items.description:
Countries: World, Afghanistan, Brazil, Colombia, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ecuador, India, Iran (Islamic Republic of), Lebanon, Mali, Mexico, Myanmar, Nigeria, occupied Palestinian territory, Pakistan, South Sudan, Sudan, Syrian Arab Republic, Ukraine
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

Launch of Global Platform - Attacks on Health Care in Conflict
03 May 2026 marked ten years since the adoption of UN Security Council Resolution 2286 on protecting health care in conflict. To mark this milestone, Insecurity Insight launches an interactive global platform documenting attacks on health care.

Data from Insecurity Insight shows:

  • Over 18,000 attacks on health care recorded since 2016 across 51 countries
  • Annual incidents have increased more than eightfold between 2016 and 2024 (470 in 2016 → 3,920 in 2024)
  • More than 3,800 health workers killed
  • Rising use of drones and explosive weapons driving mounting damage to health facilities and death of medical personnel

Explore any thematic areas (shown on the left) either globally or for a specific country by clicking on the country.
By health service affected:

  • At least 482 maternal health services have been damaged or disrupted by violence or coercive measures, affecting mothers, newborns, and entire communities.
  • Also available for affected countries: See for example Ukraine, Sudan or any other country by clicking on the country on the map.
  • The increasing use of explosive weapons in populated areas has caused extensive damage to health infrastructure. The growing use of drones has also become a significant factor, with more than 1,000 recorded incidents affecting health care delivery. See for example Myanmar
  • Violence against health care has also been observed to surge during disease outbreaks, including COVID-19, Ebola, cholera, and mpox, further undermining public health responses. See Outbreak
  • In addition, health workers and facilities are frequently targeted during episodes of political violence, particularly when treating injured protesters. See Political
  • Vaccination initiatives have also been subject to violent attacks. See Vaccination

Save the Date: 3 June: 11 am EST / 4 pm BST / 5 pm CET
Join us for a 90-minute webinar for an evidence-based look at the state of health care protection in conflict settings a decade after UNSC Resolution 2286. Sign up here

Past editions: 01-14 April; 18-31 March; 04-17 March; 18 February-03 March; 04-17 February; 21 January-03 February; 07-20 January; 24 December-06 January; 10-23 December; 26 November-09 December; 12-25 November; All

SHCC Reports: 2024; 2023; 2022; 2021; All

Help support the protection of health care by sharing this resource. Please copy and paste this link: https://bit.ly/15-28Apr2026Health

SHCC Attacks on Health Care
This section aligns with the definition of attacks on health care used by the Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC).

Africa
Democratic Republic of the Congo
18 April 2026: In Masisi town and territory, North Kivu province, an MSF nurse of Masisi General Referral Hospital was shot twice in the chest and killed in his home by a gunman in military uniform in the evening. Source: MSF

Mali
25 April 2026: In Kati commune and cercle, Koulikoro region, a research physician and biostatistician of the University Clinical Research Centre (UCRC), was killed during an attack on the area by armed insurgents while he was going to dawn prayer. Sources: Al Jazeera and Order of Physicians of Mali

Nigeria
15 April 2026: Along the Gusau–Anka road, between Anka and Gusau LGAs, Zamfara state, the Head of Pharmacy at General Hospital Nasarawa was abducted by bandits. Source: Bakatsine

22 April 2026: In Wawa community, Borgu LGA, Niger state, a doctor was abducted from a private hospital by gunmen who fired in the air. A relative reported that the gunmen demanded a ransom of N150 million for his release. Sources: PM News and The Nation Newspaper

South Sudan
07 April 2026: Update: In Lankien town, Nyirol county, Jonglei state, two MSF aid workers who were considered missing following a government airstrike on an MSF hospital 03 February, were believed to have died. Source: MSF

07 April 2026: Update: In Jonglei state, MSF were able to confirm the status of the 26 out of 291 staff in Lankien and Pieri, who were missing following the escalation of the conflict in Jonglei during February and March 2026, including one who was killed. Source: MSF

Sudan
Data from Insecurity Insight shows that since fighting started in Sudan between the SAF and RSF in April 2023 and 14 April 2026:

  • Over 745 attacks on health care recorded
  • 206 health workers killed, 15 kidnapped and 126 arrested
  • Health facilities damaged over 200 times
  • 51 attacks on maternal health care services
  • 55 attacks where armed drones affected health care

Incident briefs: 15-28 April; 01-14 April; 18-31 March; 04-17 March; 18 February-03 March; 04-17 February; 21 January-03 February; 07-20 January; 24 December 2025-06 January; 10-23 December; All

Asia
Afghanistan
22 April 2026: In Taloqan city, Takhar province, doctors at Takhar Specialised Hospital were threatened with knives by armed men in the paediatric ward. Ten people, including six doctors, were injured. Source: Zawia News

India
17 April 2026: In Khurai Lamlong area, Imphal city, Imphal West district, Manipur state, three ambulance drivers were dragged from their vehicles and physically assaulted with fibre sticks by Rapid Action Force (RAF) members of the police after being stopped at a checkpoint while on their way to pick up patients injured during a violent demonstration. Source: ACLED1

Myanmar
Data from Insecurity Insight shows that since the military coup in Myanmar on 01 February 2021 and 14 April:

  • 1918 attacks on health care recorded
  • 173 health workers killed and 909 arrested
  • Health facilities damaged over 497 times
  • 53 attacks on maternal health care services
  • 81 attacks where armed drones affected health care

Incident briefs: 15-28 April; 01-14 April; 18-31 March; 04-17 March; 18 February-03 March; 04-17 February; 21 January-03 February; 07-20 January; 24 December 2025-06 January; 10-23 December; All

Pakistan
15 April 2026: In Dera Murad Jamali area, Nasirabad district, Balochistan province, a police officer was killed when armed men riding three motorcycles opened fire on a polio team which had stopped under a shaded area for lunch. Sources: Dawn I and The Print

15 April 2026: On Samana Road, Hangu district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province, a police officer was shot and injured when a polio team was attacked by an armed individual. The officer was taken to hospital, and the suspect was arrested shortly after the incident. Source: The Print

15 April 2026: In Dawar colony, Jhandu Khel locality, Bannu district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province, three polio workers from BHU Ghazi Marjan were abducted by suspected militants while administering polio vaccines to children. Police immediately reached the scene, cordoned off the area and started a search operation to recover the abducted polio workers; they were found the following day. Source: Dawn I and Dawn II

17 April 2026: In Urmar village, Peshawar suburb, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province, a 35-year-old polio worker was shot and killed by unidentified gunmen during an ongoing polio eradication campaign. Source: Rediff

21 April 2026: On Sharea Faisal highway, Karachi city and division, Sindh province, a doctor serving at a private hospital in Gulistan-e-Jauhar was shot four times and killed by unidentified armed men at night. The doctor was returning home from Cantt Station in a rickshaw along with his wife. Source: Geo News

21 April 2026: In Quetta city, Balochistan province, a female student was abducted from the Bolan Medical College female hostel by Pakistani security and taken to an undisclosed location. Source: Prokerala

25 April 2026: In Rashkai, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province, an ambulance was fired upon by unknown perpetrators during a rescue attempt. Source: Rediff

Europe
Ukraine
21 April 2026: In Sumy city, raion and oblast, the roof of a medical facility was hit and damaged by Russian drone strikes. Sources: Dankor Online, Debaty Sumy, Inkorr, Interfax Ukraine, Kordon Media, Sumy Oblast Police and Sumy OVA

24 April 2026: In Bilozerka rural settlement, Kherson raion and oblast, a 59-year-old and a 68-year-old female nurse were injured in a Russian drone strike while walking down the street at 0720hrs. The latter nurse also sustained shrapnel wounds to her arm and leg. Sources: ACLED1 and Suspilne

At least2,962 attacks on health care in Ukraine have been documented by Insecurity Insight since the start of Russia’s full-scale invasion in February 2022, making it one of the most dangerous conflicts for patients' accessing health care. In these incidents, 458 health workers were killed and 423 injured. Health facilities have been damaged over 1,554 times. Explore thisinteractive map (available in English and Ukrainian) to see where incidents took place between 24 February 2022 and 31 March 2026.

Middle East and North Africa
Iran
22 April 2026: In Abdanan city and county, Ilam province, a Kurdish health worker of Rasoul Akram Hospital was arrested by Iranian forces and transferred to Mahpareh Prison in Dareh Shahr County. The details of his detention and charges were not disclosed by security or judicial authorities. Source: Hengaw Organisation for Human Rights

Lebanon
Due to the very high number of reported events in Lebanon during the reporting period, the corresponding data is available on request.

Occupied Palestinian Territory
Data from Insecurity Insight shows that since 07 October 2023 and 14 April 2026:

  • Over 3252 attacks on health care recorded
  • 777 health workers killed and 673 arrested1
  • Health facilities damaged over 471 times
  • 61 attacks on maternal health care services
  • 150 attacks where armed drones affected health care

Incident briefs: 15-28 April; 01-14 April; 18-31 March; 04-17 March; 18 February-03 March; 04-17 February; 21 January-03 February; 07-20 January; 24 December 2025-06 January; 10-23 December; All

Syrian Arab Republic
Around 17 April 2026: In Jaramana city, Rif Dimashq district and governorate, a young doctor from As-Sweida city was found dead in his home with multiple stab wounds, reportedly killed by unidentified gunmen. Source: ACLED1

The Americas
Brazil
24 April 2026: In Pedrinhas neighbourhood, Juazeiro do Norte city, Ceara state, a man was shot and killed at a rehabilitation clinic for substance abuse by five unidentified armed men who broke into the building, turned off the electricity, and fired at him. Source: ACLED1

Colombia
26 April 2026: In Santander de Quilichao, Cauca department, an ambulance from Eduardo Santos Hospital in La Unión was intercepted and attacked by unidentified gunmen, who stole medical equipment and essential supplies, leaving the vehicle out of service. Consequently, a Yellow Alert for hospitals was declared in Nariño to reinforce care and protect healthcare personnel. Source: Pulse

Mexico
20 April 2026: In Vista Alegre neighbourhood, Acapulco city, Guerrero state, a woman was killed and two others were injured after being shot by an unidentified perpetrator, outside the Clinical Analysis Laboratory of the Autonomous University of Guerrero (UAGro) during a protest at around 1850hrs. Source: Quadratin

22 April 2026: In Minatitlán city, Veracruz state, a man was abducted after being shot in the parking lot of Petróleos Mexicanos (PEMEX) Regional Hospital, following a car chase which ended at the hospital. It is reported that around 12 shots were fired and caused distress to patients and staff. Sources: Mega Noticias and El Diario

Global Health Insecurity
These events go beyond the definition used by the SHCC to cover a wider range of events that affect the provision of health care including events perpetrated by patients or relatives of patients, private individuals and criminals as well as significant strike action or protests by health care personnel.

The Americas
Ecuador
23 April 2026: In Rocafuerte and Eloy Alfaro streets, Portoviejo city, Manabi province, a man was killed during a raid on a clinical laboratory by at least two unidentified armed perpetrators. Source: ACLED1

23 April 2026: In Pascuales neighbourhood, Guayaquil city, Guayas province, two pharmacies were hit and damaged by explosives detonated by two unidentified armed perpetrators travelling on a motorcycle and dressed in black, in an attack described as reprisals for refusing to pay extortion. Source: ACLED1

items.title: Syria: Building Back Together: NGOs as Partners in a Recovery that Leaves No One Behind | 11 May 2026
items.description:
Country: Syrian Arab Republic
Source: Syria NGO Forum

Please refer to the attached file.

The Syria NGO Forum welcomes the High-Level Political Dialogue between the European Union and the Syrian Transitional Government, and the inaugural Syria Partnership Coordination Forum, taking place on 11 May 2026. This dialogue reflects the increased space and opportunity for deeper engagement between the international community and the Syrian government, as well as the expanding possibilities of working toward recovery, reconstruction, and an inclusive transition.

The Syria NGO Forum emphasizes the urgency of ensuring that this opening translates into tangible progress for the millions of Syrians who remain in acute need, and that the recovery architecture now being built reaches the most vulnerable and leaves no one behind. As the scale of need remains immense, over 15.6 million people require humanitarian assistance, displacement continues to affect millions inside and outside the country, and the conditions for safe, voluntary, and dignified return are not yet in place across large parts of Syria, we have a critical imperative to shape Syria's recovery in ways that are inclusive, principled, and grounded in field realities.

The Syrian government's Statement of Recovery Priorities for International Cooperation is a welcomed step in that direction, signaling commitment to reconstruction and the restoration of public services at scale. The Syria NGO Forum welcomes this direction. At the same time, we also see important areas where the framework can be developed and strengthened—in the priorities it covers, and in how these are meant to be implemented—to ensure that recovery reaches the most vulnerable and does not come at the cost of addressing immediate humanitarian needs.

Since 2011, NGOs and community-based organisations have been at the forefront of the response, operating under highly constrained conditions to save and sustain lives and livelihoods across Syria. The Syria NGO Forum’s 188 member organisations collectively mobilise over $800 million annually in support of the humanitarian, early recovery, and development response across Syria, working across nearly every sub-district in Syria and every sector of the response. That presence is now a valuable asset for Syria's recovery. Today, as the focus shifts toward recovery, development, and peacebuilding, NGOs are ready to be partners in that effort—bringing community trust, technical expertise, and ground-level reach that a broad-based, inclusive recovery will require if it is to succeed for all Syrians.

A recovery that builds back better and leaves no one behind requires that NGOs are engaged as partners from the outset; that humanitarian and recovery efforts are sustained together rather than treated as sequential; that recovery priorities are broad-based, cross-cutting, and inclusive; and that the enabling environment is in place for principled, effective delivery. These principles shape the analysis and recommendations that follow.

Pillar 1: Engaging NGOs as Strategic Partners in Syria’s Recovery

Syria's NGO community is uniquely positioned to accelerate inclusive recovery, building on technical expertise, field presence, and community relationships. NGOs have already been delivering across each of the government's four priority areas, from restoring infrastructure and basic services to social and economic resilience and systems-strengthening. Syrian NGOs in particular bring community trust, deep reach, and contextual understanding to ensure that recovery efforts connect to the communities who are furthest from services, and least visible to national planning processes. We call on the Syrian government and international donors to: 1

● Recognise NGOs as co-shapers and partners of recovery, not only implementers of it. The current framework articulated in the Statement of Recovery Priorities positions NGOs as implementers rather than strategic partners. Yet this approach underutilises one of Syria's key assets: over 3,000 Syrian NGOs that have spent more than a decade building knowledge, community trust, human capital, and mobilisation capacity. NGOs should be engaged in the design, monitoring, and accountability of recovery programming from the outset—shaping decisions through structured, meaningful engagement. NGOs bring several comparative advantages that are directly relevant to shaping recovery: their field presence and community relationships can surface and amplify the voices of communities that have long been disconnected from services and state planning processes; their technical expertise can help translate high-level recovery priorities into operational plans, including through meaningful contribution to line ministry implementation plans; and their reach and regular community engagement positions them to monitor progress toward recovery goals at the field level where it matters most.

● Build on years of early recovery work already underway. Much of what the government's Recovery Priorities aspire to—livelihoods support, basic service restoration, local capacity building, community resilience—is work that NGOs have been doing throughout the conflict, often under extremely constrained conditions and with limited resources. This track record should be the starting point for recovery partnerships. Emerging coordination structures like the Solutions Working Group, bringing together NGOs and UN agencies, should also be engaged effectively to leverage existing capacities. Bypassing existing NGO capacity risks duplication, inefficiency, and the erosion of gradual progress that has taken years to build.

● Leverage the NGO community's comparative advantage in reaching the most vulnerable. NGOs and civil society organizations are an essential channel through which displaced populations, minority communities, women and girls, persons with disabilities, and other disenfranchised groups access services and support. A recovery architecture that does not deliberately build on this reach will not reach everyone. The EU should ensure that its recovery programming explicitly draws on and strengthens NGO engagement with these populations as a structural asset for inclusive recovery.

● Sustaining and strengthening Syrian NGOs as the backbone of the response. Syrian national NGOs have been the backbone of the humanitarian response for over 14 years, building deep community trust, strong contextual knowledge, and solid institutional capacity under extraordinarily difficult conditions. Yet they continue to face structural barriers that undermine their sustainability: limited direct funding, heavy compliance burdens, short grant cycles, and intermediary arrangements that restrict their decision-making power. A genuine shift toward localisation requires equitable and direct funding, reduced administrative barriers, meaningful inclusion of Syrian NGOs in decision-making, and recognition of their leadership in shaping the direction of Syria’s development.

Pillar 2: Sustaining Humanitarian Assistance as an Enabler of Recovery

Syria has a unique opportunity to bring humanitarian, development, and peace efforts together to advance recovery in a coordinated and sustainable manner. At the same time, the country remains in an immediate post-crisis phase, with acute and widespread humanitarian needs driven by conflict, recurrent natural hazards, and regional escalation. With over 15.6 million people still in need, humanitarian assistance will be an essential enabler of inclusive recovery in Syria. The Syria NGO Forum stresses that safeguarding principled, needs-based humanitarian space is not in tension with recovery ambitions, but rather, makes those ambitions achievable for all Syrians. We call on the EU and international donors to:

● Sustain humanitarian financing in parallel with scaling recovery investment. Humanitarian assistance sustains lives and livelihoods in vulnerable communities while recovery conditions are being built and 2 development scales up. Prematurely redirecting resources away from humanitarian assistance does not accelerate that transition—it undermines it, by cutting off the support that communities depend on before the systems that would replace it are in place. And while engagement from development actors has grown significantly, the corresponding funding has not yet materialised at the scale required, leaving needs unmet in the interim. Consequences of shrinking humanitarian funding are already visible: funding cuts have produced service gaps in camps across Raqqa and Hasakah, where families are losing access to food, clean water, and healthcare, and where malnutrition cases are projected to rise as a direct result. These are also areas that fall outside the current priority areas of the government's “No Camps” implementation plans—meaning that durable solutions and return pathways for these communities are not yet defined, and the need for sustained humanitarian support remains a present and ongoing reality.

● To ensure returns are voluntary, safe, and dignified, invest in accelerating the enabling environment, not the pace of return. Syria is witnessing growing pressure, both internal and external, to accelerate the return of internally displaced persons and refugees from neighbouring countries, despite conditions on the ground that do not yet support large-scale, safe, and sustainable return. Across large parts of Syria, enabling conditions including mine clearance, adequate housing, restored basic services, livelihoods, and efforts to support social cohesion are not yet in place. Returns are already happening, with over 1.6 million since 2024. But where returns outpace enabling conditions, families risk secondary displacement or are left without adequate shelter, services, or livelihoods to rebuild their lives. This experience does not stay contained: potential returnees are already being discouraged by the struggles of family members who returned and could not rebuild. Resources and political will must be invested in accelerating enabling conditions that make return safe, voluntary, and durable.

● Decouple camp closure from political timelines and anchor it in durable solutions. The Syrian government's "No Tents, No Camps" initiative is a welcome and necessary vision for moving towards a Syria in which displacement is no longer the lived reality of over one million people. However, pursuing this vision on a political timeline, without the programmatic architecture and financing to support it, carries serious risks. There are approximately 1,500 camps and IDP sites across Syria housing over one million people, three quarters of whom are women and children. Closing sites before enabling environments are in place—whether due to political priorities or lack of funding for solutions—will lead to deeper and more protracted vulnerability. Any implementation plan for camp closure must be anchored in a durable solutions framework that accounts for mine action, property rights, livelihoods, protection, and social integration, not in political timelines. Pillar 3: Broadening A Recovery Framework That Leaves No One Behind The Syria NGO Forum welcomes the Syrian government's Statement of Recovery Priorities as an important step toward structured, large-scale investment in Syria's recovery. NGOs are committed to contributing across the four priority areas: restoring critical infrastructure, resuming basic services, building social and economic resilience, and developing core systems and public administration. At the same time, 14 years of operational experience across Syria and lessons learned from comparable contexts globally leads us to identify areas of the framework that need to be further developed and strengthened—both in content and in implementation—to ensure that recovery is inclusive and reaches those who have been most marginalised by conflict. It is with a commitment to leaving no one behind in Syria’s recovery that we offer the following analysis and recommendations, and urge the Syrian government and the EU to consider:

● Mine action must be recognised as a foundational enabler of recovery. The current framework makes minimal references to mine action, yet landmine and explosive remnants of war contamination is one of the most immediate physical barriers to return, agricultural recovery, and infrastructure rehabilitation across large parts of Syria. Without clearance, land cannot be farmed, roads cannot be 3 safely used, and communities cannot go home. Since December 2024, more than 2,000 casualties due to explosive ordnance have been recorded, including 748 people killed. Mine action in Syria is a prerequisite for recovery, and should be resourced as such. The EU and Member States should consider what a level of ambition commensurate with the scale of contamination in Syria would look like—drawing on lessons from other contexts, including Ukraine—and ensure coordinated investment across instruments and donors.

● Strengthen the framework's approach to displacement and return. The Recovery Priorities do include a commitment to promoting voluntary, safe, and dignified return within a durable solutions framework, and to addressing housing, land, and property issues within national legal frameworks. However, with 5.5 million IDPs inside Syria, addressing displacement and reintegration—including local integration, as another solution pathway alongside return—will be one of the central challenges of recovery, requiring dedicated analysis and planning as a cross-cutting dimension of the framework. Concretely, the framework does not yet address the specific barriers that prevent return at the community level: the absence of mine clearance, unresolved property and tenure disputes, and the financial inability of families to rebuild. Documentation is a particular gap—only 37 percent of Syrian families currently have full legal identity documents for all members, a barrier that affects not only return but access to services, legal rights, and economic participation. Civil documentation for those inside and outside the country must be considered as a core enabler of recovery; the EU should advocate for its prioritisation and support dedicated programming in this area.

● Protection and social cohesion require explicit attention. The framework should also account for the protection risks that accompany recovery—including property and tenure disputes, social cohesion risks, and the potential for harm if returns are accelerated without the right conditions. Localised violence, identity-based conflict, and the spread of hate speech also create risks for families and communities and undermine Syria’s recovery. Where social cohesion dialogue is needed, civil society organisations are often best placed to lead these processes than government actors—reinforcing the need for partnership with such actors as part of a broad-based approach to recovery.

● Persons with disabilities must be treated as a cross-cutting dimension, not a sub-category. The framework mentions disability only once, under the social protection pillar. Yet disability must be mainstreamed across all sectors—health, education, livelihoods, and infrastructure—ensuring that as systems are built back, they are also built to be accessible.

● Clarify the operationalisation of the Recovery Priorities, engaging NGOs as partners in that process. A critical next step will be translating the vision advanced by the Recovery Priorities into operational reality and mainstreaming these priorities across ministerial strategies. Operationalising these priorities will require concrete, sequenced implementation plans; monitoring and accountability frameworks that track progress at the community level; and consultation and feedback mechanisms to adapt priorities as the context evolves. With deep experience translating strategic objectives into community-level delivery, NGOs have direct and practical contributions to make to each of these steps. Independent civil society participation is also essential to ensure accountability to the communities the framework is meant to serve. The EU should press for NGOs to be meaningfully engaged in this process, as active contributors to operationalising a recovery vision.

● Frame the Recovery Priorities as a living document, and ensure its development is genuinely consultative. The Statement of Recovery Priorities should represent a starting point, with space to evolve, adjust, and adapt. As the framework is developed further, including through line ministry implementation plans and sectoral strategies, it is essential that this process is inclusive, transparent, and grounded in community perspectives. The EU should encourage the Syrian government to establish structured, ongoing consultation mechanisms as the Recovery Priorities are operationalised, 4 ensuring that the voices of NGOs, local civil society, and communities are a consistent input into a framework that will need to adapt as Syria's recovery unfolds.

Pillar 4: Creating An Enabling Environment for Principled Delivery

Creating an effective enabling environment for delivery of humanitarian, recovery, and development assistance is an urgent prerequisite for implementation of these priorities. Yet across Syria, international and Syrian NGOs continue to face complex administrative, regulatory, and financial constraints that create delays, absorb capacity, and undermine timely and principled delivery. Despite some positive developments in recent months, the operational environment remains deeply challenging: lengthy registration processes, extensive documentation requirements, slow and inconsistent approvals, financial transfer obstacles, and unpredictable access conditions collectively impede the sustained, needs-based delivery that recovery requires. We urge the EU to engage on these issues directly with Syrian authorities:

● Safeguard independent humanitarian space within the recovery framework and Syria’s transition. The recovery plan cannot function as a mandatory operating framework for all actors and assistance. In areas facing severe needs or exposure to recurrent shocks, and places where government access is limited or contested, humanitarian actors must retain the freedom to respond to needs based on humanitarian principles, independently of recovery priorities and government structures. Yet current procedures—including approval requirements for movements and meetings, requests for personal staff data, and approval authority over NGO local partners—all create risks for independent delivery of assistance. In Syria’s current context, a distinct humanitarian operational space must be explicitly recognised and protected from administrative and political encroachment. The EU should advocate clearly for this in its engagement with Syrian authorities.

● Streamlining registration and project approval processes, enabling principled and effective service delivery: NGOs currently navigate multiple layers of approval through the International Cooperation Department (ICD), line ministries, and ICDs at the field level, with requirements that often vary across geographical areas. New project approval requirements introduced in 2026 requiring detailed information-sharing and instituting lengthy approval timelines are delaying humanitarian assistance, with serious consequences for both relief and recovery. A key priority should be streamlining approval processes between national and field level, as well as harmonizing processes across governorates. The investment of time and resources that NGOs currently spend navigating administrative procedures comes directly at the expense of rapid, needs-based intervention.

● Ensure consistent, principled, and unimpeded humanitarian access across all of Syria. While conditions have improved in many areas, access remains uneven, unpredictable, and shaped by fragmented governance arrangements, particularly in areas of complex or contested control. This results in inconsistent procedures, approval delays, and varying interpretations of requirements, which directly affect the ability of humanitarian actors to plan, scale, and sustain operations. These access constraints also undermine recovery interventions, which depend on predictable, sustained presence and the ability to operate across locations without disruption. We therefore call for clear, consistent, and harmonized access across all areas of Syria, ensuring equitable access to affected populations regardless of location.

● Ensure that currency and banking policies do not disrupt delivery of humanitarian and recovery programming. The transition away from USD transactions creates operational risks for programme delivery. Uneven and restricted Syrian pound liquidity across geographic areas and banking channels, gaps between official exchange rates and the parallel market rates, evolving and rapidly-shifting regulatory guidance, and inconsistent interpretations of regulatory guidance across commercial banks are all affecting the ability of NGOs to pay staff, procure goods, and deliver cash-based programming to 5 the communities that depend on it. We encourage the EU to raise with Syrian authorities the importance of ensuring that the pace of monetary policy transition does not outrun the readiness of the financial system to support it, and to find mechanisms that can ensure continuity of humanitarian and recovery assistance within broader financial system reforms.

● Scale up and restructure financing to deliver on Syria's recovery. Syria's recovery requires a fundamental shift in how international assistance is structured and delivered. In practice, while there has been a significant push toward recovery and increased engagement from development actors, corresponding funding has not yet materialized at the scale required. The growing gap between expectations and financial realities is placing impossible demands on a humanitarian system to deliver on development outcomes. The Syria NGO Forum urges the EU to lead across three dimensions:

○ Scale Up. The volume of development and recovery financing must increase substantially and urgently to match the scope of Syria's needs and the ambition of its Recovery Priorities. Political commitment to Syria's transition must be backed by commensurate financial commitment.

○ Restructure. Single-sector, short-term, earmarked funding cycles are structurally inadequate for recovery, peacebuilding, and market development. The EU should prioritise instruments that can enable multi-year, flexible, area-based, and multi-sector programming that spans the humanitarian-development nexus. Multi-year commitments are especially critical for giving Syrian NGOs the planning horizons to sustain operations, retain staff, and be full participants in Syria’s recovery.

○ Direct funding equitably. A genuine commitment to Syrian-led recovery requires that Syrian NGOs have meaningful access to financing—not only through intermediary partnership arrangements that reduce their decision-making power and absorb resources, but through more direct funding channels that recognise their capacity, their community relationships, and their centrality to any recovery that is locally owned and sustainable.
